questions.question: Why can average fleet utilization hide a serious partitioning problem?
options: Because one partition can be overloaded while the rest of the fleet stays mostly idle.
Because averages automatically include p99 latency and queue depth details.
Because utilization graphs cannot be collected in distributed systems.
Because hot partitions only happen in single-region databases.
questions.explanation: Skew is about concentration, so fleet averages often look safe while one owner is failing.

questions.question: What is the first thing to clarify about a hotspot?
options: Which router version is installed on every engineer laptop.
Whether the heat is caused by reads, writes, a tenant, a key, or a time pattern.
How many markdown files document the service.
Whether the API uses JSON or Protobuf.
questions.explanation: Mitigation depends on the actual source of concentration, so diagnosis comes first.

questions.question: Which mitigation best fits a read hotspot on a small set of objects?
options: Cache, request coalescing, or replica fanout on the hot read path.
Randomly deleting half the objects during peak traffic.
Replacing all APIs with batch exports once per day.
Disabling observability to reduce metric overhead.
questions.explanation: Hot reads usually respond well to read-path mitigations, not schema destruction.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of salting or splitting a hot write key?
options: It spreads writes, but reads and aggregation become more complex.
It guarantees every future migration is free.
It removes any need for backpressure or retries.
It makes the hottest key stronger for transactional semantics automatically.
questions.explanation: Write spreading helps concentration, but reconstruction and read locality often get harder.

questions.question: Which metric most directly reveals noisy-neighbor behavior in a multi-tenant shard pool?
options: Per-tenant share of shard traffic and latency compared with fleet medians.
The number of files in the deployment manifest.
The total count of source code comments.
The length of incident titles in the pager system.
questions.explanation: Noisy neighbors are exposed by per-tenant concentration and degraded neighbor performance.

questions.question: Why is "add more shards" often an incomplete answer to hotspotting?
options: Because concentrated traffic may keep hitting the same logical owner and not spread at all.
Because sharding is unrelated to performance or availability.
Because more shards force every query to become strongly consistent.
Because caches stop working once shard counts increase.
questions.explanation: If routing logic or key choice stays the same, added shards may not help the hot owner.

questions.question: If retries are amplifying a partition incident, what is the best next move?
options: Use backoff, retry budgets, and admission control before the overloaded owner collapses further.
Encourage clients to retry faster so the partition catches up.
Turn off all alarms to avoid confusion.
Move every tenant in the system immediately without a plan.
questions.explanation: Retry amplification is an overload problem, so rate discipline and shedding matter.

questions.question: What makes a senior answer stronger when discussing hot partitions?
options: Connecting the hotspot cause to a targeted mitigation and an explicit observability plan.
Saying that modern distributed databases solve skew automatically.
Avoiding any mention of noisy neighbors or tail latency.
Talking only about average throughput and hardware size.
questions.explanation: Strong answers connect diagnosis, mitigation, and detection rather than naming generic tools.
